title: Desensitization
section: The Immune System in Health and Disease

items.subtopic: Desensitization | DRUG ALLERGY AND THE NEED FOR DRUG DESENSITIZATION | DRUG ALLERGY DEFINITIONS AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Understanding the Need for Drug Desensitization in Allergic Patients
items.content: Drug desensitization (DD) is a critical treatment modality for patients with drug allergies, particularly when alternative medications are less effective or unavailable. This approach leverages immune inhibitory mechanisms to safely administer small, sequential doses of the allergenic drug until therapeutic levels are achieved.
keyPoints: 10–20% of the world's population engaged in health care claims to have a penicillin allergy.
Drug desensitization protocols show outstanding safety and efficacy for infections, chronic inflammatory diseases, malignancies, and other conditions.
items.reference: Desensitization · Desensitization | DRUG ALLERGY AND THE NEED FOR DRUG DESENSITIZATION | DRUG ALLERGY DEFINITIONS AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Desensitization | DRUG ALLERGY AND THE NEED FOR DRUG DESENSITIZATION | DRUG ALLERGY DEFINITIONS AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Risk Factors and Definitions of Drug Allergy
items.content: Drug allergy, synonymous with drug hypersensitivity, involves acute and delayed symptoms post-medication exposure. Risk factors include female gender, specific HLA haplotypes, atopy, polypharmacy, older age, chronic diseases, microbiome changes, and drug-dependent factors like administration route and excipients.
keyPoints: Drug allergy is associated with factors such as female gender, specific HLA haplotypes, atopy, polypharmacy, older age, chronic diseases, and microbiome changes.
Single-dose oral challenges are safe for low-risk penicillin allergy patients.
items.reference: Desensitization · Desensitization | DRUG ALLERGY AND THE NEED FOR DRUG DESENSITIZATION | DRUG ALLERGY DEFINITIONS AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: CLASSIFYING HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS | PHENOTYPES, ENDOTYPES, AND BIOMARKERS
items.title: Diagnosing Type I Hypersensitivity Reactions: The Role of Tryptase Levels
items.content: Type I hypersensitivity reactions are characterized by the activation of mast cells and basophils through IgE-dependent or -independent mechanisms. These reactions can manifest with symptoms such as itching, flushing, hives, angioedema, dyspnea, wheezing, oxygen desaturation, throat tightening, nausea, vomiting, and hypotension. Elevated serum tryptase levels are a specific biomarker for diagnosing these reactions.
keyPoints: Type I hypersensitivity involves mast cell and basophil activation via IgE-dependent or -independent mechanisms.
Symptoms include itching, flushing, hives, angioedema, dyspnea, wheezing, oxygen desaturation, throat tightening, nausea, vomiting, and hypotension.
Tryptase levels above 11.4 ng/mL or a patient's baseline × 1.6 within 30 min to 4 h of symptoms are diagnostic for type I reactions.
Baseline tryptase levels ≥7.5 ng/mL may indicate hereditary alpha tryptasemia (HaT), affecting anaphylaxis severity in 4–6% of Caucasian populations.
items.reference: Desensitization · CLASSIFYING HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS | PHENOTYPES, ENDOTYPES, AND BIOMARKERS

items.subtopic: CLASSIFYING HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS | PHENOTYPES, ENDOTYPES, AND BIOMARKERS
items.title: Understanding Delayed Type IV Hypersensitivity Reactions: Identifying Contraindications for Drug Desensitization (DD)
items.content: Delayed type IV hypersensitivity reactions are categorized based on their presentation and severity. Benign maculopapular rashes without systemic symptoms may be amenable to drug desensitization, whereas severe cutaneous adverse reactions with systemic symptoms (SCARS), such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), are contraindications for DD due to the risk of triggering severe reactions.
keyPoints: Delayed type IV reactions include benign maculopapular rashes and severe cutaneous adverse reactions with systemic symptoms (SCARS).
Benign rashes without systemic symptoms may be suitable for drug desensitization.
SCARS, such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), are contraindications for DD due to severe reaction risks.
items.reference: Desensitization · CLASSIFYING HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTIONS | PHENOTYPES, ENDOTYPES, AND BIOMARKERS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS
items.title: Basophil Activation Test (BAT) for IgE Sensitization
items.content: The Basophil Activation Test (BAT) is a valuable diagnostic tool that assesses IgE sensitization by exposing the patient's basophils to the suspected drug in vitro, thereby avoiding the risks associated with direct skin testing.
keyPoints: Eliminates need for direct skin testing which carries a small risk of anaphylaxis
Provides evidence of IgE sensitization without requiring exposure to the allergen
items.reference: Desensitization · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS
items.title: Desensitization Mechanisms for IgE-Mediated Reactions
items.content: IgE-mediated desensitization utilizes inhibitory pathways in mast cells and basophils activated by low doses of drug antigens, which recruit phosphatases to block signal transduction and prevent mediator release.
keyPoints: Inhibits IgE receptor signaling via recruitment of phosphatases
Protects against anaphylaxis during desensitization process
items.reference: Desensitization · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS
items.title: Desensitization Protocols for Type I Reactions
items.content: Type I reaction desensitization protocols involve low starting doses with multiple incremental steps, typically using three bags of 1/100, 1/10, and undiluted concentrations to reach the target dose in approximately 5.7 hours.
keyPoints: Protocols start with low doses and progress by doubling
Target dose reached in about 5.7 hours using a standardized three-bag protocol
items.reference: Desensitization · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS

items.subtopic: INDICATIONS (FIG. 365-2) | DD BREAKTHROUGH REACTIONS AND OUTCOMES | ANTIBIOTICS
items.title: When to Consider Drug Desensitization for Antibiotics
items.content: Drug desensitization (DD) is indicated for patients with IgE-dependent and independent reactions, CRRs, mixed, and nonsevere delayed type IV phenotypes. ST-positive patients require DD due to high anaphylaxis risk upon reexposure. Controlled drug challenges are suitable for mild reactions with negative biomarkers.
keyPoints: DD is indicated for IgE-dependent and independent reactions, CRRs, mixed, and nonsevere delayed type IV phenotypes.
ST-positive patients require DD due to high anaphylaxis risk upon reexposure.
Controlled drug challenges are suitable for mild reactions with negative biomarkers.
Outpatient settings can handle the majority of DD, reducing treatment time and costs.
items.reference: Desensitization · INDICATIONS (FIG. 365-2) | DD BREAKTHROUGH REACTIONS AND OUTCOMES | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: INDICATIONS (FIG. 365-2) | DD BREAKTHROUGH REACTIONS AND OUTCOMES | ANTIBIOTICS
items.title: Managing Breakthrough Reactions During Drug Desensitization
items.content: Breakthrough reactions (BTRs) are typically mild and occur in 10–30% of protocols, requiring symptom-specific management. BTRs do not prevent protocol completion in 99% of cases, with no reported deaths due to DD. Comorbidities increase risk during BTRs.
keyPoints: BTRs occur in 10–30% of protocols and are typically mild.
Symptom-specific management is required for BTRs, including epinephrine for severe reactions.
BTRs do not preclude protocol completion in 99% of cases.
No deaths due to DD have been reported.
Comorbidities such as pregnancy and decreased lung function increase risk during BTRs.
items.reference: Desensitization · INDICATIONS (FIG. 365-2) | DD BREAKTHROUGH REACTIONS AND OUTCOMES | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: CHEMOTHERAPY
items.title: Reducing Acute Reactions to Taxanes with Premedication
items.content: Premedication with antihistamines and corticosteroids significantly reduces acute reactions in patients receiving paclitaxel or docetaxel, from over 40% to about 1%. This is crucial as these reactions are often due to lipid excipients like Cremophor and polysorbate 80, which can cause IgE sensitization.
keyPoints: Over 40% of patients exposed to paclitaxel and docetaxel initially presented with acute reactions.
Premedication reduces these reactions to approximately 1%.
Reactions are often due to lipid excipients (Cremophor, polysorbate 80) causing IgE sensitization.
items.reference: Desensitization · CHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: CHEMOTHERAPY
items.title: Desensitization Protocols for Taxanes
items.content: Desensitization protocols are effective in managing drug desensitizations (DDs) to paclitaxel and docetaxel, with high completion rates. Initial reports showed that 20% of cases experienced breakthrough reactions (BTRs), but subsequent studies demonstrated successful management with mild BTRs in a significant portion of patients.
keyPoints: First DD to paclitaxel reported in 2002.
Early study: 940 DDs in 138 patients, 20% had BTRs; 22% returned to standard infusions.
Review of 25 studies with 976 patients and 2396 DDs showed completion rates of 95–100%, mild BTRs in 32% for paclitaxel and 20% for docetaxel, with no deaths.
items.reference: Desensitization · CHEMOTHERAPY

items.question: What is one of the benefits of drug desensitization (DD) as mentioned in the passage?
options: It eliminates the need for any medication.
It allows patients to use medications they are allergic to by using immune inhibitory mechanisms.
It reduces the cost of all medications.
items.explanation: Drug desensitization allows patients to use medications they are allergic to by utilizing immune inhibitory mechanisms.

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS
items.assertion: Desensitization protocols for type I reactions involve multiple steps with dose doubling.
items.reason: The inhibitory pathways in mast cell models guide the generation of human protocols with low starting doses and multiple steps that progress by doubling the dose administered in the previous step at constant time intervals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why desensitization protocols for type I reactions involve multiple steps with dose doubling.
items.reference: Desensitization · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS

items.assertion: Steroids protect against anaphylaxis during desensitization.
items.reason: Premedications are tailored to the initial symptoms, and steroids do not protect against anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because steroids do not protect against anaphylaxis; however, the reason is true.
items.reference: Desensitization · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING | DEFINITIONS AND MECHANISMS | PROTOCOLS

items.assertion: Patients with delayed rashes to paclitaxel can develop acute reactions including anaphylaxis upon subsequent exposures.
items.reason: Delayed rashes indicate underlying IgE sensitization that may manifest as more severe reactions on re-exposure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why delayed rashes can lead to acute reactions upon subsequent exposures.
